name: Source Analysis Skills
description: How to evaluate, analyse and use historical sources effectively in exam answers
commonMisconceptions: A biased source is not automatically useless - it can still reveal attitudes and intentions of the author
Primary sources are not automatically more reliable than secondary sources
Describing what a source says is not the same as analysing it - analysis requires inference and evaluation
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 All papers: Source and interpretation questions
ocrRef: OCR A (J410) / OCR B SHP (J411): Source evaluation questions

name: Medicine Through Time
description: The development of medicine from ancient times to the present day, including key individuals and breakthroughs
commonMisconceptions: Jenner did not understand why vaccination worked - he observed the effect but germ theory came later
The Four Humours theory was not just medieval - it persisted well into the early modern period
Pasteur proved germs cause disease; Koch identified specific germs for specific diseases - they are different contributions
The NHS was not universally welcomed in 1948 - many doctors initially opposed it
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section A: Medicine through time, c1250-present (Thematic Study)
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 1: Medicine in Britain, c1250-present (thematic study)
ocrRef: OCR B SHP (J411): History of medicine

name: Elizabethan England
description: Life in Elizabethan England 1568-1603, including government, religion, exploration and daily life
commonMisconceptions: Elizabeth did not have absolute power - she relied on Parliament for taxation and the Privy Council for governance
The defeat of the Armada was not solely due to English skill - weather and Spanish errors played major roles
The Religious Settlement was a compromise, not a victory for either Protestants or Catholics
Not all Elizabethans attended the theatre - it was primarily popular with Londoners
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section B: Elizabethan England, c1568-1603
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 2: Early Elizabethan England, 1558-88 (British depth study)

name: Weimar & Nazi Germany
description: Germany 1918-1945: the Weimar Republic, rise of the Nazis, life in Nazi Germany and the Holocaust
commonMisconceptions: Hitler did not seize power by force in 1933 - he was appointed Chancellor legally through democratic processes
The Treaty of Versailles was not the sole cause of the Weimar Republic's problems - there were many contributing factors
Not all Germans supported the Nazis - there was significant opposition and resistance throughout the period
Hyperinflation of 1923 and the Great Depression of 1929 were separate economic crises with different causes
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 3: Weimar and Nazi Germany, 1918-39 (modern depth study)
ocrRef: OCR B SHP (J411): Germany in transition, 1919-39

name: Cold War
description: Superpower relations and the Cold War 1941-1991, including key crises and the collapse of the Soviet Union

subskills.name: The End of the Cold War

commonMisconceptions: The Cold War was not just between the USA and USSR - it involved proxy wars and alliances across the globe
Detente did not mean the end of rivalry - both sides continued the arms race and competed for influence
The Berlin Wall fell in 1989, but the Soviet Union did not collapse until 1991 - these are separate events
The Cuban Missile Crisis was not resolved by one side backing down - it involved mutual compromise
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 1 Section B: Conflict and tension between East and West, 1945-1972
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 2: Superpower relations and the Cold War, 1941-91 (period study)
note: Date range differs by board: AQA covers 1945-1972, Edexcel covers 1941-91, OCR A covers 1941-1991

name: Norman Conquest
description: The Norman Conquest of England 1065-1087, including the Battle of Hastings and Norman control
commonMisconceptions: Harold was not killed by an arrow in the eye - this is likely a myth from the Bayeux Tapestry which may show a different figure
William did not win at Hastings simply because he was a better leader - luck, timing and Harold's exhaustion all played a part
The feudal system was not a rigid pyramid - relationships were complex and varied across regions
The Domesday Book was for taxation purposes, not simply a record of who owned what land
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section B: Norman England, c1066-c1100
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 2: Anglo-Saxon and Norman England, c1060-88 (British depth study)
ocrRef: OCR B SHP (J411): The Norman Conquest, 1065-1087

name: American West
description: The American West c1835-c1895, including settlement, conflict and the experience of Native Americans
commonMisconceptions: Native Americans were not a single group - there were hundreds of distinct nations with different cultures and languages
Manifest Destiny was not a universally held belief - many Americans opposed westward expansion
Cowboys were not all white American men - many were Black, Mexican or Native American
The Battle of Little Bighorn was a Native American victory, but it ultimately led to increased US military action
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 1 Section A: The American West, c1835-c1895 (Period Study)
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 2: The American West, c1835-c1895 (period study)
ocrRef: OCR A (J410): The Making of America, 1789-1900

name: Power & the People
description: How people have challenged authority and fought for rights throughout British history
commonMisconceptions: The Magna Carta did not grant rights to ordinary people - it protected the rights of barons against the king
The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was not a complete failure - it contributed to the eventual end of serfdom
Suffragettes and suffragists were different groups with different methods - not all campaigners used militant tactics
The Chartists did not achieve their demands immediately, but five of their six points were eventually enacted
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section A: Power and the people, c1170-present (Thematic Study)
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 1: No direct equivalent (thematic study options differ)
ocrRef: OCR A (J410): Power, the People and Democracy in Britain

name: Crime & Punishment
description: How crime and punishment have changed from medieval times to the present day
commonMisconceptions: Trial by ordeal was not random - it was believed to be God's judgement, so it had religious significance
The Bloody Code did not mean everyone convicted was executed - many sentences were commuted to transportation
Prisons were not always intended as punishment - before the 19th century they were mainly holding places before trial
The death penalty was not abolished in the UK until 1969 (for murder) - not during the Victorian era
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section A: Crime and punishment, c1000-present (Thematic Study)
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 Paper 1: Crime and punishment in Britain, c1000-present (thematic study + historic environment)
ocrRef: OCR B SHP (J411): Crime and punishment through time

name: Essay Technique & Argumentation
description: How to write effective GCSE History essays with clear arguments, evidence and balanced evaluation
commonMisconceptions: A good conclusion does not simply repeat what you have already written - it must offer a clear overall judgement
You do not need to agree with the statement in the question - what matters is the quality of your argument
Listing facts is not the same as analysis - you must explain WHY evidence supports your point
Balance does not mean giving equal weight to both sides - you should prioritise the stronger argument
aqaRef: AQA 8145 All papers: Extended writing questions
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1HI0 All papers: Extended response questions
ocrRef: OCR A (J410) / OCR B SHP (J411): Structured and essay responses

name: Chronological Knowledge
description: Understanding timelines, periods and how to place events in their correct historical context
commonMisconceptions: Events do not happen in isolation - students must link causes and consequences across time periods
A turning point does not mean everything changed overnight - it means the direction or pace of change shifted
Short-term causes are not always more important than long-term causes - context determines significance
The Victorian era and the Industrial Revolution are not the same period - they overlap but are distinct
ocrRef: OCR A (J410) / OCR B SHP (J411): Chronological knowledge and understanding

name: Historical Interpretations
description: Understanding why historians disagree and how to evaluate different interpretations of the past
commonMisconceptions: An interpretation is not the same as a source - interpretations are constructed arguments about the past, sources are evidence from the past
Two different interpretations can both be valid if they are supported by evidence
Historians do not disagree because one is wrong - they may focus on different evidence or ask different questions
You should not simply describe what each interpretation says - you must compare and evaluate them
aqaRef: AQA 8145 Paper 2 Section B: Historic environment and interpretations
ocrRef: OCR A (J410) / OCR B SHP (J411): Interpretation and analysis questions
